title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 37:1-11 Jacob lived in the land where his father had stayed, the land of Canaan. This is the account of Jacob's family line. Joseph, a young man of seventeen, was tending the flocks with his brothers, the sons of Bilhah and the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives, and he brought their father a bad report about them. Now Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons, because he had been born to him in his old age; and he made an ornate robe for him. When his brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of them, they hated him and could not speak a kind word to him. Joseph had a dream, and when he told it to his brothers, they hated him all the more. He said to them, “Listen to this dream I had: We were binding sheaves of grain out in the field when suddenly my sheaf rose and stood upright, while your sheaves gathered around mine and bowed down to it.” His brothers said to him, “Do you intend to reign over us? Will you actually rule us?” And they hated him all the more because of his dream and what he had said. Then he had another dream, and he told it to his brothers. “Listen,” he said, “I had another dream, and this time the sun and moon and eleven stars were bowing down to me.” When he told his father as well as his brothers, his father rebuked him and said, “What is this dream you had? Will your mother and I and your brothers actually come and bow down to the ground before you?” His brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the matter in mind.
weekTheme: This week has one big idea: to help you understand what Ease is. There is nothing inherently wrong with enjoying ease. Who doesn’t love taking it slow, relaxing, being comfortable, or dealing with minimal stress? But a life of ease stops us from growing up and being able to handle the inevitable adversity that life throws at us. Too much ease and we will get stuck in immaturity. What you are called to become and what this study is designed to help you do is learn to embrace difficulty and not retreat back to ease, so you can live a rich and rewarding life of growth, joy, confidence, and strength.

days.title: Loved
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:3 Now Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons, because he had been born to him in his old age; and he made an ornate robe for him.
days.reflection: Being loved is one of the most important things a young man can know. But it’s not something we often think about consciously when it comes to our families. Your mom or dad saying “I love you” can feel like something they are supposed to say, not something that moves you or excites you. We often prefer to be liked or loved by a person we think is cute or we are “into.” However, even if it doesn’t feel like some incredible thing, without a sense of love, a young man will struggle and strive to try and get people to like him. This can cause pain, a sense of being misunderstood, and a feeling of being left out. Being loved is an important part of being a man, and being loved is the first thing we learn about Joseph. In fact, he was loved more than all his brothers. This is going to create some problems later on, but at this point, it has made his life easy. He is loved because he was born to a Father who wanted him very much. His Dad, Israel, didn’t think he could have any more kids, so when Joseph came along, he was overjoyed. This love was so real and so well known, that it is the first thing we read about Joseph's life. “Israel loved Joseph.” That is how his story begins. The author of Genesis is trying to tell us something important here. It’s not just a random detail. Before Joseph will go on to be second in charge of all of Egypt, before he will have to resist temptation, before he will be put in prison, he is a person who is loved. This wasn’t just true of Joseph; it’s true of you. The truest thing about you and the first thing you need to know about yourself is that you are loved. You are loved by the person spending this time with you today, and more importantly, you are loved by God himself. In the New Testament, one of Jesus' disciples wrote this about being loved by God: "See what great love the Father has lavished on us, that we should be called children of God! And that is what we are!" (1 John 3:1) Just like Joseph's Dad loved and wanted him, your heavenly Father loves and wants you. That is who you are.
questions: Who do you think is the most loving person you know? What makes him or her so loving, and how can you feel that love? Why do you think it’s important for a person to start their life journey by feeling that they are loved? What do you think would happen if they don’t? Take a moment to think, don’t just blurt something out, but is there a time when you felt really loved in your life? What was the situation and how did you respond?

thoughts.verse: See what great love the Father has lavished on us, that we should be called children of God! And that is what we are!
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you with any changes you are currently struggling with. Ask God to steady your heart and give you peace.

days.title: Favor
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:3 Now Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons, because he had been born to him in his old age; and he made an ornate robe for him.
days.reflection: One definition of favor goes like this: “to feel or show approval or preference for something or someone.” Joseph had favor from his father. This means that his father had a special place in his heart for him, a special preference toward him. You probably have your own favorite things. Your favorite foods, favorite music, favorite video games. These are the things that you prefer over all the other options. We know what it is to have favorites, but do we ever really see ourselves in this light? Do you see yourself as favored? This passage tells us a lot about how Joseph was seen in the world. He wasn’t just loved. There was a sense of favor on him. He was shown that he was special when he was given an amazing coat. This coat was an outward symbol and expression from his dad's heart of his value in the world. Whenever anyone saw him wearing it, they knew he was loved by his father. The thing about having favor is that it's not something you can earn. It's something that comes from who you are. Think about how you came into the world. What did you do when you were a baby? Did you do any chores around the house? Do you earn any money to help pay the mortgage? Did you help with the electric bill or the wifi? No. Here is what you did: You slept, woke up, cried, pooped, ate, slept, dribbled on stuff, pooped again, went back to sleep and then did it all again in the middle of the night where your parents had to wake up, deprive themselves of sleep to make sure you were ok. Then you did this day after day, week after week, month after month. Yet your mere existence made your parents completely reorder their lives, schedules and priorities around you. You didn’t even have the ability to say thanks. They simply loved you for who you are, not for what you could do. That’s the power of favor. And that’s what Joseph teaches us. We live our lives from favor, not for favor. Joseph wasn’t loved because of what he did for his dad, he was loved because of who he was to his dad. From favor, not for favor – this is the pattern of God's life. This is the central truth of the gospel Christians believe: God doesn’t love us because we are good and moral or because we try hard and never make mistakes. Romans 5:6-8 reminds us of this. “You see, at just the right time, when we were still powerless, Christ died for the ungodly. Very rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though for a good person someone might possibly dare to die. But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: While we were still sinners, Christ died for us.” There was nothing Joseph could do to make his father love him anymore or any less. And there is nothing you can do to make your heavenly father love you any more or any less. Favor comes from God; I hope you learn to enjoy and experience it. But… as you will start to see, favor from God doesn’t mean favors from God. We will explore that a bit more tomorrow.
questions: Quick answers. What is your favorite meal, music, sports team, teacher, subject? Why?
If you could pick an item of clothing to show that you were special, what would you want it to be and why?
Ask your Dad to share one of his favorite memories about you when you were a baby.

thoughts.verse: For his anger lasts only a moment, but his favor lasts a lifetime; weeping may stay for the night, but rejoicing comes in the morning.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Spend some time thanking God for being kind and merciful and for showing us favor because of his love, not because of our efforts at trying to be good or earn it.

days.title: Naivety
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:1,4 1 Joseph, a young man of seventeen, was tending the flocks with his brothers, the sons of Bilhah and the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives, and he brought their father a bad report about them. 4 When his brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of them, they hated him and could not speak a kind word to him.
days.reflection: One of the hardest parts of growing up and becoming a man is understanding the consequences of our actions and taking into consideration how to relate to others in our world. We can make decisions with good intentions, but make decisions with bad impact. This inability to think on a larger level and understand the dynamics of life is called being naïve. To be naive means to lack experience, wisdom or judgment. You can see this naïvety when you talk with a 5- or six-year-old. For them, things are quite simple, but you can see, even at your age, that they don’t know how life really works on a larger level. As a teenager, we can see that Joseph was naïve. He didn’t take into account how others would feel about the favor in his life, or his need to manage his relationships with his brothers. Because he enjoyed being loved, he didn’t see that this could make his brothers jealous, or that he would need to have gained favor with them. We read that he gave a bad report to his dad about how the brothers were doing with their job of shepherding, and he showed off his coat in a way that made him feel special, but oh how they felt left out. Moving out of ease and embracing difficulty will mean dealing with the difficulty of relationships. People can be easily hurt, and those around you may not be having the same experiences as you. You need to take into account how others may be impacted, not just how well you are doing. Just because you are deeply loved and shown favor in your life doesn’t mean that you can walk around arrogantly and think that you are better than people or that you don’t need to be aware of what those around you are going through. Your decisions will always impact other people’s realities. Failure to do that will lead to conflict. Even in the early part of Joseph's story, you can see the conflict building in his family. Jesus was not naïve. He was the master of understanding where people were coming from, what they wanted, and what they were feeling. At times, he refused to entrust himself to people. Other times, he was moved with compassion to help people. Joseph was naïve, and it caused him great pain. To move forward in life, you are going to need to embrace the challenges of relationships, and gain wisdom beyond your years. You are already doing this by spending time right here on the Primal Path, so way to go.
questions: In 1 Corinthians 13:11, the Apostle Paul writes this, “When I was a child, I talked like a child, I thought like a child, I reasoned like a child. When I became a man, I put the ways of childhood behind me.” How has some of your thinking changed since you were a little kid? What did you think like then, and how do you think differently now?

Can you think of a moment of unintended consequences in your life? Meaning, you wanted one thing to happen, but you didn’t take into account that something else would happen. (Dad, you may want to share a story here.)
If you were one of Joseph's brothers, how do you think you would have felt if he walked around in his sweet new coat and told his dad a bad report about you?

thoughts.verse: The wisdom of the prudent is to give thought to their ways, but the folly of fools is deception.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Is there an area in your life where you need wisdom so you don’t act naively? School, friends, family relationships? James 1:5 says “If any of you lacks wisdom, you should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault, and it will be given to you." Spend some time thanking God that he gives wisdom when we ask for it, and ask for help in the specific area you need it in.

days.title: Importance
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:6-11 6 “Listen to this dream,” he said. 7 “We were out in the field, tying up bundles of grain. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles all gathered around and bowed low before mine!” 8 His brothers responded, “So you think you will be our king, do you? Do you actually think you will reign over us?” And they hated him all the more because of his dreams and the way he talked about them. 9 Soon Joseph had another dream, and again he told his brothers about it. “Listen, I have had another dream,” he said. “The sun, moon, and eleven stars bowed low before me!” 10 This time he told the dream to his father as well as to his brothers, but his father scolded him. “What kind of dream is that?” he asked. “Will your mother and I and your brothers actually come and bow to the ground before you?” 11 But while his brothers were jealous of Joseph, his father wondered what the dreams meant.
days.reflection: When we are babies, we tend to think that the whole world revolves around us. It can get even worse in the toddler years. Toddlers cry when they don’t get what they want and completely melt down if they are told to take a nap or even if something as small as a toy is taken away from them. They have a massive sense of self-importance for such a little person. The same kind of thinking happens, but at a different level, with teenagers. When you are going through the teenage years, you can begin to get a sense of your own potential and importance in the world. Things begin to become meaningful to you, and you can start to really dream about what you want out of life and who you could become. God will often begin to place dreams in people’s hearts when they are teenagers. In fact, God loves to speak and call teenagers and give them a sense of their destiny. Joseph is just 17 years old at this point in his story. Daniel was just a teenager when he was taken to Babylon, Mary, the mother of Jesus, was a teenager when God called her, and the disciples in the New Testament were teenagers when Jesus called them to leave everything and follow him to change the world. It can be a really amazing feeling to know that we are capable of doing great things in the world, and there are seeds of greatness within us, but we have to learn to hold the tension of greatness with the gift of humility. Joseph was right about the dream he was given, but he didn’t have the maturity to share it in a way that his brothers would understand. In fact, he assumed his sense of calling was about himself, and didn’t understand that God had called him for the sake of others, not the sake of himself. He is going to have to learn a lot about servanthood and greatness in the coming days. Jesus' disciples often had fights about which one of them was to be considered the greatest (Luke 22:24). Yet, Jesus had to teach them that greatness in the kingdom of heaven is different than greatness in the eyes of the world. Greatness is not about ease and comfort for our own good, it's about sacrifice and servanthood for the sake of others. It's ok to have desire and vision for your life, but it's probably wise not to blurt it out to anyone and everyone. Sometimes it will be misunderstood. Sometimes it will get you in trouble.
questions: Have you ever seen someone get arrogant and boast? How did it make you feel about them? Have you ever been a bit of a showoff yourself? How did others treat you?
Have you ever had a sense of what your dream for your life may be, or a picture of the future that gets you excited? Or maybe just something you are really looking forward to?
What is it, and why does it excite you?
Have you ever had someone jealous of you for any reason? How did this show up in your relationships or group of friends? What happened because of it?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. The wise don’t make a show of their knowledge, but fools broadcast their foolishness. Proverbs 12:23 Even fools are thought wise when they keep silent; with their mouths shut, they seem intelligent. Proverbs 17:28 Those who control their tongue will have a long life; opening your mouth can ruin everything. Proverbs 13:3 Take control of what I say, O LORD, and guard my lips. Psalms 141:3 Here are some verses about the importance of learning to control what we share. Which one of these do you like the most? Why? Read through the one you like the most slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you from it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he has a plan for your life and that you have a destiny and a purpose. Ask him to reveal more of that to you, and give you wisdom on how to share it.

days.title: Goodbye Ease
days.scripture: GENESIS 37:3-11 3 Now Israel loved Joseph more than any of his other sons, because he had been born to him in his old age; and he made an ornate robe for him. 4 When his brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of them, they hated him and could not speak a kind word to him. 5 Joseph had a dream, and when he told it to his brothers, they hated him all the more… 8 His brothers said to him, “Do you intend to reign over us? Will you actually rule us?” And they hated him all the more because of his dream and what he had said… 10 When he told his father as well as his brothers, his father rebuked him and said, “What is this dream you had? Will your mother and I and your brothers actually come and bow down to the ground before you?” 11 His brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the matter in mind.
days.reflection: Did you notice the intense situation building in Joseph's story so far? Read the verses above and see if you can pick out the most intense words. Why can’t all stories happen without drama, pain and conflict? Why does hardship have to leak into everything? Why can't our stories be easier? If we could write Joseph's story how we wanted, I bet we would write it something like this. Joseph was born to his dad later in life and his dad was really grateful for that, so he really loved him a lot. He got him a really special coat that was really fashionable and everyone was really happy for him. God gave him some cool dreams; they all came true. The girl, the job, the cool car, house and vacations. He lived happily ever after and when he died, he went to heaven. The end. But you know - just by looking around and remembering the things you have already been through - that life isn’t about ease. Sure, we can have moments when things are going well and we are really happy. But life simply refuses to stay like that. To handle the reality of life, we are going to have to learn to shift from craving ease to embracing difficulty. This can be really hard, and, at times, it can feel intimidating and overwhelming. It can make you want to shrink back and retreat to a place of comfort. But you won’t become the man you want to be if you do that all the time. Because life is hard, we need to learn to embrace overcoming difficulties. Joseph is going to leave his place of comfort and be thrust into a world of difficulty. He is going to have to overcome the difficulties of betrayal, slavery, slander, being forgotten, imprisonment, forgiveness, and legacy. God will be with him, and he is going to become a remarkable man that God can actually use so those dreams become a reality. And not in a way that simply makes him happy, but in a way that will help a ton of people. You may wish that your story could be an easy one too. But embracing difficulty and having the determination needed to overcome it can help you develop into a strong and resourceful man.
questions: What is something difficult that you have already had to face? Did you overcome it?
How did it make you feel when you had done that?
How do you feel about the idea of shifting from “ease to difficult” as you grow up?
What excites you about that? What makes you nervous?
How have you seen your family overcome difficulties?

thoughts.verse: We can rejoice, too, when we run into problems and trials, for we know that they help us develop endurance. And endurance develops strength of character, and character strengthens our confident hope of salvation. And this hope will not lead to disappointment. For we know how dearly God loves us, because he has given us the Holy Spirit to fill our hearts with his love.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Spend some time asking God to give you courage to learn to embrace the difficult challenges of life that are coming your way, and thank him for the help in getting through them so far.

extras: WEEK 1 MANSCHOOL FAVORITES AND FAVOR This first man school night is low key and easy. Man School is a way over the next few years to implement and encode different fun life lessons. And we want to start easy to get our feet wet! To be honest, the man school parts I did with my son are some of my favorite memories to this day. This week is called favorites and favor. Get some intel on what your son's favorite things are. Ask friends, mom, or make a list yourself. Favorite food, favorite place to go, favorite activity, favorite dessert, etc. Then, stack a night or time this weekend to do them together. Use this time to let him know that you love him, that you are glad that he was born, that you love him as your son, and that you delight in him. And you can vocalize you are doing it to show him you love him, but also that you care and notice. You want some of his best joyful memories in his teenage years to include you on a lot of them. For a bonus, you may want to go back to day 2 where he answered the question, ‘If you could pick an item of clothing to show that you were special, what would you want it to be and why?’ and maybe pick it up for him as a surprise. The goal of this time is simply to connect, establish the tradition, and let your son know he is loved. Fill him up and celebrate him for a hard first week! And get him geared up for week 2.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
